Kemetic Glossary

Words of power from the ancient tongue

mdw nṯr writings rooted in Africana scholarship: Obenga 2004, Karenga 2004, Carruthers 1995, Diop 1991, ben-Jochannan, and the Senebty Cultural Consensus Panel 2026. Western Egyptology (Allen 2014, Faulkner 1962, Nunn 1996) consulted only as cross-reference where it does not contradict the Africana lineage.

Your Sound Journey

Phase 1: Ankh Seeds — Learn the sounds of the scribes

𓊝 The Blending Chamber

Combine your sounds to build words — like a scribe assembling a scroll

𓆣 Word Hunt — Find Sounds in the Wild

Build This Word
𓊝

Parent & Guardian Guide

How to practice Per Ankh Beginnings with your child

🏛️ The Per Ankh Method

Per Ankh Beginnings follows the ancient Egyptian scribal school (Per Ankh — "House of Life") approach to learning, adapted for modern phonics. Your child progresses through two phases:

  • Phase 1 — Ankh Seeds: Learn each sound through a culturally meaningful anchor image (e.g., B = Baobab). Tap the card, hear the sound, then say it aloud.
  • Phase 2 — Blending Chamber: Once 10+ sounds are mastered, unlock word-building. Hear each sound separately, then blend them together into a word.

⏱️ Session Length

Young learners (ages 4–7) focus best in short bursts. Aim for 5–10 minutes per session, 3–5 times per week. Stop before your child gets frustrated — it's better to end on a win than push through fatigue.

🎯 How to Practice a Sound

  1. Tap the card to open the phoneme detail.
  2. Press "Hear" together — listen to the sound and the anchor word.
  3. Say the sound together — exaggerate the mouth shape. For /b/, feel the lips pop. For /s/, feel the hiss.
  4. Press "Say It" to let your child try alone. The app uses gentle scoring (40% threshold) — praise effort, not perfection.
  5. Connect to the image: "B is for Baobab — the big tree that grows tall! Can you say /b/ like the baobab?"

🌟 Tips for Success

  • Multi-sensory learning: Trace the letter with a finger while saying the sound. Draw it in sand or rice. Find the sound in everyday words.
  • Sound, not name: Teach the sound /b/ (a quick puff), not the letter name "bee." This makes blending much easier later.
  • 3–5 sounds per session: Introduce 1–2 new sounds per session, then review 2–3 familiar ones. Don't rush — mastery matters more than speed.
  • Celebrate with the culture: "You've unlocked the sound of the Scarab! The scribes of Kemet would be proud."
  • Use the anchor stories: Each sound connects to a sentence. Read it together. Point to the anchor word. This builds meaning alongside phonics.
  • Quiet environment: The microphone works best in a quiet room. Background noise can confuse the speech recognition.

🔗 Blending Practice (Phase 2)

Once your child knows 10+ sounds, the Blending Chamber unlocks:

  1. "Each Sound" — plays the sounds separately: /s/ ... /u/ ... /n/
  2. "Blend It!" — plays the full word: "sun"
  3. Have your child stretch the sounds first (ssss-uuuu-nnnn), then speed up until they hear the word.
  4. Use your finger to slide under each sound slot as they say it — this builds left-to-right reading flow.

💡 If Your Child Gets Stuck

  • Press "Hear" again — repetition is learning, not failure.
  • Say the sound together — model it clearly and slowly.
  • Move to a different sound and come back later — no pressure.
  • The app auto-helps after 7 seconds of silence — it will pronounce the word and move on.
  • Some sounds are naturally harder (/th/, /r/, /ch/) — these develop with age. Don't worry if they take longer.

Remember: The ancient scribes of the Per Ankh learned through patience, repetition, and mentorship. You are your child's first teacher — their Seba. Every sound they learn is a seed that grows into the power of reading. 𓊝

DETERMINATIVE
HEAR, SAY, SEE
Listen carefully...
WORD HUNT

Timeline of Ma'at

The great civilizations of Africa, from the first pyramids to the city of books

Trade Routes of Africa

How the great civilizations of Africa connected through trade

Why Trade Routes Matter

Long before modern borders existed, African civilizations were connected by vast networks of trade and cultural exchange. Merchants, scholars, and craftspeople carried more than goods — they carried ideas, languages, technologies, and spiritual practices across the continent.

The Nile Valley corridor linked Kemet and Kush for over 3,000 years. Trans-Saharan caravans exchanged West African gold for Saharan salt. The Swahili coast connected Africa to India and China. And interior networks — from the Niger-Benue rivers through the Congo Basin to the Copperbelt and Great Zimbabwe — formed a chain of exchange that united the continent from west to east.

These routes prove that Africa was never isolated. It was always a continent of movement, connection, and shared knowledge — balance, reciprocity, and righteous exchange between peoples.

☥ Pert em Heru: Beginnings
The Book of Coming Forth by Day — First Steps

The oldest spiritual text in human history — over 5,000 years old. This is not a "book of the dead." It is a book of LIFE — a guide for the soul's journey through the Duat and its triumphant emergence into eternal light. Narrated by Seba Khafre.

☥ Afrocentric Scholarly Foundation

Grounded in the work of leading Afrocentric scholars:

  • Maulana Karenga — Maat: The Moral Ideal in Ancient Egypt
  • Cheikh Anta Diop — African Origin of Civilization
  • Theophile Obenga — African Philosophy: The Pharaonic Period
  • Asa Hilliard III — SBA: The Reawakening of the African Mind
  • Jacob Carruthers — Mdw Ntr: Divine Speech
  • Molefi Kete Asante — The Egyptian Philosophers
𓋹

Per Ankh Senebty

The House of Health

𓋴𓎟 Hem-Sba

Senebty, young one. The path is long. We do not pretend.

The Morning of Khaemwaset

Walk the Threshold once. Then enter the Foundations.

The Eight Foundations

Senebty has eight Foundations. Each one is a daily iri — a thing you do, not a thing you read. The first is open. The others are being prepared. Walk what is open. Return for the rest.

  1. 1
    Mu — Water
    Treasure: Water · Iri: WATER_IRI
    One cup. One iri. The Nile in your khat.
    Open
  2. 2
    The Four Treasures — Body
    Treasure: Khat · Iri: BODY_IRI
    A daily somatic check across the four treasures.
    Open
  3. 3
    Tjau — Extended Breath
    Treasure: Tjau · Iri: BREATH_IRI
    The breath the sunu examines first.
    Open
  4. 4
    Mu — Streak
    Treasure: Mu · Iri: STREAK_IRI
    Twenty-one days. Memory becomes body.
    Open
  5. 5
    Wedeha — Photo
    Treasure: Khat · Iri: PHOTO_IRI
    The plate of Kemet — whole foods, Maat in nutrition.
    Open
  6. 6
    Hesi — Voice
    Treasure: Hesi · Iri: VOICE_IRI
    The voice trained, the throat opened.
    Open
  7. 7
    Senedjem — Joy
    Treasure: Senedjem · Iri: CREATION_IRI
    A daily making-sweet, an iri of joy.
    Open
  8. 8
    Heka — Words of Power
    Treasure: Speech · Iri: TEACHING_IRI
    The first speech of the priest. Spoken aloud, it sets the world.
    Open

Africana sources: Karenga (Maat), Carruthers (Mdw Ntr), Obenga, Diop, ben-Jochannan, Hilliard, Browder, Finch (African Medicine) · Cultural Consensus Panel 2026.

𓈗

Daily Senebty Ritual · Step 1 of 4

Sema breath

Sixty seconds. Let your breath find Maat.

Power Word

Today’s word: SENEB

Speak it three times. Slow, full breath. The word is carried by your voice.

Four Treasures

How is each treasure today?

Khat (body)
Ib (heart-mind)
Ka (vital essence)
Ba (personality-soul)

Seal

The ritual is done. Tap the seal to close it.

The Trials are being prepared. The path remembers you. Return when the door opens.

The door is the entrance to Imhotep's mortuary chapel at Saqqara. Imhotep was deified in the New Kingdom and Ptolemaic veneration; his Old Kingdom titles in the cult are pedagogical anchors, not biographical claims (per Africana primaries — Karenga, Obenga, Hilliard).

☥ Yeshua’s Way
The Galilean of the Kemet Continuum — Twelve Chapters

Yeshua ben Yosef — the teacher of Galilee — walked a land still shaped by Kemet. Before Rome’s eagles, before Greek letters, Galilee and Judea lived inside the Pharaonic world: trade with On (Heliopolis), Nubian scholars in the great houses, the river-flood calendar, the temple mysteries. This telling draws on Africana and African scholarship only. It names him as he was: a dark-skinned Afro-Asiatic teacher carrying Maat into a country weighed down by Isfet — foreign occupation, temple corruption, broken reciprocity.

Journey Progress 0 / 12 chapters

☥ Africana & African Scholarly Foundation

These tellings draw on oral tradition and the work of African and Africana scholars — no European sources are used for characterization:

  • Yosef A.A. ben-Jochannan (Dr. Ben) — African Origins of the Major “Western Religions”
  • John Henrik Clarke — Christopher Columbus and the Afrikan Holocaust; lectures on Yeshua as African teacher
  • Cheikh Anta Diop — Civilization or Barbarism; cultural unity of Kemet and surrounding zones
  • Théophile Obenga — African Philosophy: The Pharaonic Period
  • Asa G. Hilliard III — SBA: The Reawakening of the African Mind
  • Zora Neale Hurston — Moses, Man of the Mountain; oral recovery of African scripture
  • Ivan Van Sertima — African Presence in Early Asia; the Afro-Asiatic corridor
🎥 Learn More Library
Curated videos for parents — watch & learn

A living feed from six channels doing the deep work of reclaiming African history: Without History, TrillBlk, Building Se7en, Kamjiverse, Tapvideo, and The Kings Monologue. Click any video to watch on YouTube — these are adult-facing so the kids' reading app stays focused on stories & Maat.

GLOSSARY
Seba Khafre
Breathe with the circle...
The circle grew and shrank. Two movements — each one needing the other. What were the two partners you felt?
Name both partners separated by "and" — like "growing and shrinking"
The Kemetu called this Sema — the joining of two things that cannot be what they are alone. You just felt it in your own body.
